mnemonics.title: INSERT Sorted Card Hand
mnemonics.content: Imagine sorting a hand of playing cards: pick up each card and insert it into its correct position among the cards already held - that's exactly how Insertion Sort works!

mnemonics.title: Loop Invariant Memory
mnemonics.content: INVARIANT: Left side stays sorted after each pass - like a growing sorted pile where new element finds its home.

mnemonics.title: Complexity Pattern
mnemonics.content: Best: Already sorted → n comparisons (O(n)); Worst: Reverse sorted → n(n-1)/2 comparisons (O(n²)); Average: Random → n²/4 comparisons (O(n²)).

acronyms.expansion: Time recurrence for Insertion Sort - each of n elements processed by comparing with up to i-1 sorted elements
acronyms.usage: Used to derive Θ(n²) complexity by summation of arithmetic series: Σi from 1 to n-1 = n(n-1)/2

visualTips.concept: How Insertion Sort builds sorted array incrementally
visualTips.visualization: Imagine building a sorted line: start with first element, then for each new element, shift larger sorted elements right until finding the correct position to insert the new one.
Use vertical bar representation: [5,3,4] → mark 3 as key, shift 5 right → [_ 5 3] → insert 3 → [3 5 4]. Each pass extends the sorted portion by one element.
For reverse-sorted [n,n-1,...,1], each new element must compare with ALL previous sorted elements - like pushing through a full tube of marbles, causing maximum O(n²) shifts.

analogies.concept: Sorted porti growing one element at a time
analogies.analogy: Like organizing books on a shelf from left to right: take each book, find its correct position among already-organized books, insert it there.
Like a person sorting their desk: if papers are already mostly in order, quick adjustments suffice; if completely jumbled, much shuffling required.
Think of moving books in a shelf: instead of swapping pairs repeatedly (bubble sort), you slide a gap created by removing one book, then drop the new book in - more efficient for nearly-sorted data.
